metadata.name: Rhythm Lab Radio — Seed List for AI Music Curator
metadata.description: Reference tracks defining 'Rhythm Lab Radio quality.' Each represents the show's genre-fluid, discovery-focused, culturally grounded aesthetic. Use to calibrate recommendations: new finds should feel like they belong alongside these selections.
metadata.curator_instructions: When evaluating a new track, compare it against this seed list. Ask: Does it share sonic DNA with at least 3-5 of these tracks? Does it exhibit the qualities noted in the WHY fields? Prioritize discovery (emerging artists) but anchor taste in the canonical references.

tracks.why: Elite lyricism over cinematic production. Rhythm Lab champions UK MCs who push the form forward with substance and swagger.
Grime veteran meets avant-soul vocalist. The kind of unexpected collaboration that defines the show's taste.
New-gen artist linking with a Tribe legend. Rhythm Lab loves intergenerational bridges in hip-hop.
Local Milwaukee talent with national-caliber production. Always flag regional connections.
Spoken-word fluidity over lush, organic production. The Chicago poetry-to-rap pipeline Rhythm Lab celebrates.
Meditative, sample-driven hip-hop that rewards deep listening. Represents the show's affinity for contemplative rap.
Backwoodz Studioz leading the literary wing of hip-hop. Complex, layered, rewards repeat listens.
Abrasive and brilliant — pushes the boundaries of what hip-hop production can be. The avant-garde edge of the show.
Pivot Gang representing Chicago's thoughtful, community-rooted hip-hop tradition.

tracks.track: Woke Up and Asked Siri How I'm Gonna Die
tracks.why: billy woods + ELUCID creating hip-hop that functions as sound art. The deep end of the pool.
Diaspora hip-hop that refuses borders. Global perspective with underground credibility.
Self-produced, melodic, conscious — the Platonic ideal of Rhythm Lab hip-hop.
Liquid flows over sophisticated production. Waters-era Jenkins embodies the show's lyrical standards.
Sage Elsesser's meditative beats and introspective bars. The intersection of art, skating, and hip-hop.
Offbeat, cerebral, and deeply human. The kind of hip-hop that makes you pause and think.
Sharp pen with cinematic production. Women MCs with elite bars are always a Rhythm Lab highlight.
Effortless vocals over warm, jazzy production. The UK soul underground at its finest.
Genre-defying artistry — soul music refracted through electronic textures. A Rhythm Lab cornerstone artist.
Emerging voice with sophisticated arrangements. Discovery-focused programming in action.
Classic soul voice reimagined through a broken beat lens. The remix culture Rhythm Lab celebrates.
New Zealand-born, London-based polymath blending soul, jazz, and electronic music seamlessly.
Pure, unhurried soul music rooted in Jamaican and West African heritage. Spiritual warmth.
Berklee-trained trio making sophisticated soul accessible without dumbing it down.
Manchester's contribution to the UK soul renaissance. Confessional songwriting with genuine warmth.

tracks.track: Girls Don't Always Sing About Boys
tracks.why: DIY soul with a point of view. Represents the independent spirit Rhythm Lab champions.
Literary references woven into lush soul — her album concepts (namesake Black and Brown artists) embody cultural depth.
Genre-resistant R&B that floats between jazz, electronic, and soul. Unpredictable and magnetic.
Piano-driven meditations that blur soul, classical, and ambient. Expanding the definition of soul music.
South London mystic channeling Erykah Badu through UK sound system culture.
East London voice pushing R&B into new sonic territory with Disclosure-adjacent production sensibilities.
West London poet laureate of gentle devastation. Bridges indie and soul audiences effortlessly.
Scottish jazz that swings between broken beat and spiritual jazz. The new UK jazz wave's overlooked corner.
Jazz Dispensary catalog bringing cinematic, soul-jazz with A Tribe Called Quest lineage.
Multi-instrumentalist bandleader making maximalist, joyous jazz that connects to hip-hop and dance.
The Melbourne quartet that taught a generation that jazz-fusion could be funky, weird, and accessible simultaneously.
Formerly Shabaka Hutchings (Sons of Kemet, The Comet Is Coming) — the most important figure in new London jazz.
Spiritual jazz with dub and cumbia undertones. Her saxophone carries the weight of diasporic memory.
Post-production jazz pioneer — live recordings remixed into beat-driven compositions. Bridges jazz and hip-hop production.
Tortoise guitarist turned jazz auteur. Quiet, looping, hypnotic compositions that reward patience.
Chicago's International Anthem label flagship artist. Raw, ecstatic, deeply spiritual free jazz.
Mercury Prize winners making jazz that fills dance floors. Afrobeat meets UK jazz energy.
Reimagining jazz through the harp — an instrument rarely centered in the genre. Elegant and surprising.
Young vibes player channeling Bobby Hutcherson while staying contemporary. Blue Note's future.
Moor Mother and band creating the most radical jazz of the era. Free jazz as political weapon.
Self-taught pianist making intimate jazz that lives in the same headspace as J Dilla beats.
London collective channeling West African highlife through UK jazz sensibilities. Warm, communal, infectious.
LA cosmic jazz connector. Collaborations spanning Kamasi Washington to ambient drone — the ultimate genre-fluid artist.
Brought jazz back to the cultural conversation with The Epic. Orchestral, spiritual, unapologetically grand — jazz as event.
Thumping, rave-informed house that never sacrifices musicality for intensity.
Collage-based electronic music built from rare vinyl — Ninja Tune at its most soulful.
Orlando Higginbottom's gift for electronic music with genuine emotional core. Pop sensibility, underground credibility.
Mexico City electronic duo blending cumbia, disco, and house. Global dance floor consciousness.
Percussive electronic music drawing from African and Latin rhythmic traditions. Polyrhythmic and hypnotic.
Sam Shepherd's PhD-in-neuroscience precision applied to deeply emotional electronic music. The gold standard.
Montreal producer who made house music safe for R&B fans and vice versa. Beat switch architect.
Kieran Hebden's decades-long project of making electronic music feel handmade and organic. Endlessly inventive.
VHS-degraded house music with a human heartbeat. The analog warmth Rhythm Lab prizes in electronic music.
Jazz drummer making electronic music that swings. The rhythm section as electronic instrument.
Tom and Ed Russell channeling 90s rave energy through modern production. Emotional, physical, cathartic.
Hamburg auteur making house music that's genuinely funny, warm, and surreal. Pampa Records visionary.
Simon Green's nature-informed electronic music. Lush, cinematic, globally influenced — a Rhythm Lab perennial.
Dan Snaith's shape-shifting project — math PhD making deeply felt dance music.
Brainfeeder's Dutch connection. Where jazz improvisation meets electronic deconstruction.
Danny Wheeler keeping the broken beat flame alive. West London's rhythmic tradition carried forward.
TJ Hertz designing sound from first principles. Club music as architecture.
Steven Ellison — Alice Coltrane's grandnephew — built Brainfeeder into the most important independent label ecosystem. Jazz lineage meets digital futurism.
Blind couple from Bamako collaborating across the continent. Living legends still pushing forward.
Brooklyn's Afrobeat institution — Fela's legacy filtered through New York multiculturalism.
Detroit's multicultural underbelly — Latin rhythms meeting Motor City futurism.

tracks.track: I Get A Kick Out Of You (feat. Jay Phelps, Max Milner)
tracks.why: The live party/movement turned recording project. Where jazz, Afrobeat, and community converge.
The man who invented Afrobeat drumming alongside Fela. Every rhythm on the show traces back to his hands.
Niger's guitar hero playing Tuareg rock with punk intensity. African rock that needs no Western translation.
Soweto collective making trance-inducing music from traditional Zulu rhythms and raw electric energy.
Otura Mun channeling Yoruba spiritual tradition through electronic production in Puerto Rico. Diaspora in real time.
Lima duo making psychedelic bass music from Andean and Afro-Peruvian source material. South American futurism.
Turntables on the Hudson founder connecting Latin, African, and electronic traditions from Brooklyn.
Eblis Álvarez deconstructing Colombian tropical music into something alien and irresistible.
Mogadishu's legendary band reissued for modern ears. The reissue culture Rhythm Lab celebrates.
Afro-Colombian group rapping in Palenquero (a Spanish-Bantu creole). Language preservation through music.
Malian singer-guitarist bridging traditional Wassoulou music with contemporary global production.
Ali Farka Touré's son meeting Houston's most genre-fluid band. Cross-cultural dialogue in real time.
São Paulo collective proving Afrobeat's global vocabulary. Brazilian rhythm section meets West African brass.
Former Egypt 80 keyboardist carrying Fela's literal torch. First-generation Afrobeat authenticity.
Genre-blurring ensemble that lives in the spaces between jazz, soul, and funk.
Atlanta artist reinterpreting Mos Def through an R&B lens. Genre translation as artistic statement.
The ultimate genre-fluid band — Thai funk, dub, soul, surf rock — all in one groove. Rhythm Lab spirit animal.
Stephen Bruner's virtuoso bass in service of songs that are funny, sad, and funky all at once. Brainfeeder royalty.
Genre as costume — rock, electronic, soul, noise all inhabited and discarded fluidly.
Self-taught violinist fusing Northeast African fiddle traditions with electronic beats and R&B vocals.
The Toronto trio that proved jazz musicians raised on hip-hop would reshape both genres.
Ghanaian-American artist making orchestral pop that defies every demographic expectation.
Multi-decade shapeshifter — funk, jazz, electronic, hip-hop production — all filtered through Afrofuturist vision.
Roberto Carlos Lange making bilingual electronic pop that's political without being didactic.
Tuba-and-drums jazz with Caribbean carnival energy and anti-colonial politics. Genre as liberation.
Brooklyn art collective making sound collage that's part jazz, part noise, part cultural criticism. Uncompromising.

tracks.track: No Blacks, No Dogs, No Irish
tracks.why: Johannesburg-to-Doncaster psych-folk-electronic. Post-colonial identity as sound.
Camae Ayewa creating sonic worlds where Black liberation, experimental noise, and poetry collide.
One-man boogie-soul-funk machine making tape-saturated grooves in his bedroom. DIY ethos incarnate.
Mica Levi collaborator making R&B stripped to its emotional skeleton. Less is everything.
Paris-based artist drawing on Iranian and Egyptian heritage for pop that sounds like nowhere and everywhere.
The north star for genre-defiant artistry. Every artist on this list who refuses categorization is walking a path Björk carved. Industrial, orchestral, digital, primal — often all at once.
The original genre-fluid jazz artist. From Miles's second quintet to Head Hunters to 'Rockit' — Herbie proved jazz musicians should chase the future, not the museum. The Rhythm Lab origin story.
The most sampled artist hip-hop never credited. Wrote Nina Simone's 'To Be Young, Gifted and Black,' made visionary solo records. His DNA is in every beat-driven jazz track on this list.
Jazz flautist who chased every global rhythm he could find — bossa nova, Afrobeat, soul, funk. The original world music DJ in jazz form.
Zawinul and Shorter building jazz from synthesizers and world rhythms. Jaco Pastorius's fretless bass rewired what rhythm sections could be.
The godfather of neo-soul before it had a name. His vibes connected jazz to funk to hip-hop — sampled endlessly because the feeling is universal.
Cosmic soul-jazz that floats between meditation and the dancefloor. His Rhodes playing is the direct ancestor of every lo-fi jazz beat today.
The Mizell Brothers' production turned Byrd's trumpet into a funk machine. Blue Note's most successful crossover — jazz that made you move without apology.
Spectrum is the hardest-hitting fusion album ever made. Panamanian-American drummer who made jazz-rock fusion feel dangerous.
Zappa collaborator turned funk-jazz architect. His synth-driven grooves are the missing link between fusion and boogie.
Rio's fusion trio fusing samba with Fender Rhodes and Moog. Brazilian jazz-funk at its most infectious — where Carnival meets Herbie Hancock.
Miles Davis student who took electric jazz into pure funk territory. A psychiatrist by day, fusion visionary by night.
Prestige Records jazz prodigy who became a funk-pop icon. Her bassline became Will Smith's 'Men in Black' — jazz DNA hiding in plain sight in pop.
Manchurian-born, Bud Powell-mentored bandleader who integrated Japanese scales and wartime memory into big band jazz. Proof that jazz was always global.
City pop royalty — lush, yacht-soul arrangements with jazz harmony and Tokyo neon glow. The sound the internet rediscovered and couldn't let go of.
The king of city pop. Steely Dan sophistication filtered through Tokyo's bubble-era optimism. His wife Mariya Takeuchi's 'Plastic Love' broke the algorithm — but Tatsuro is the architect.
Yellow Magic Orchestra co-founder who moved from tropical exotica to ambient electronics to techno-pop. Japan's Brian Eno with deeper grooves.
Self-taught Sapporo pianist whose debut became a YouTube phenomenon decades later. Beautiful, urgent piano jazz — proof that the algorithm can have taste.
Yasuaki Shimizu's new age pop project — ambient, sensual, and deeply strange. Japanese Balearic before anyone used that term.
Berklee-trained Tokyo keyboardist making modern soul with J Dilla's swing. Contemporary Japanese music that bridges city pop legacy with global neo-soul.
No Kraftwerk, no hip-hop (Afrika Bambaataa), no techno (Detroit), no electronic music as we know it. The machine that started the machines.
Japanese singer Damo Suzuki fronting German experimentalists creating grooves that predicted post-punk, ambient, and electronic music by decades. Jaki Liebezeit's drumming is a genre unto itself.
Berlin school electronic pioneers — sequencer-driven cosmic music that influenced everything from ambient to synthwave. German engineering as art form.
Chilean-German producer making minimal techno that stretches time. Berlin club culture at its most hypnotic and cerebral.
Loop-Finding-Jazz-Records turned jazz samples into clicking, pulsing minimal electronic. The German precision approach to sample-based music.
Italian library music genius — his soundtracks moved from bossa nova to psychedelic jazz to proto-electronic. The Muppets made him famous, but his deep catalog is a treasure chest.
Schema Records founder connecting Italian jazz tradition to Brazilian bossa and global club culture. Bari's gift to sophisticated dancefloors worldwide.
Naples electronic scene connecting Mediterranean warmth to global dance music. Italian groove culture reimagined for modern sound systems.
Naples duo reviving Neapolitan funk and disco traditions through modern production. Mediterranean sunshine in every groove.
The provocateur poet of French pop. His arrangements — from ye-ye to reggae to funk — proved that genre tourism could be an art form when done with genuine curiosity.
French avant-garde vocalist who recorded with the Art Ensemble of Chicago. Where Parisian chanson meets free jazz — the most Rhythm Lab thing France ever produced.
French-American artist channeling Tropicália and lovers rock through a cinematic lens. Warm, unhurried, transportive.
Pi'erre's production for Playboi Carti and solo work represents the melodic, spacious side of trap — beatmaking as atmosphere. French musical DNA in Atlanta hip-hop.
Brazil's godfather of soul — brought Motown to Rio and created something entirely new. His Rational Culture albums are psychedelic-soul masterpieces. Every Brazilian funk artist owes him everything.

The inventor of samba-rock. Connected African rhythms to Brazilian carnival to American funk in ways nobody else could. Rod Stewart took his melody for 'Do Ya Think I'm Sexy' — the original is infinitely better.
His self-titled 1972 album flopped in Brazil, became a holy grail for crate diggers worldwide. Orchestral Brazilian soul that MF DOOM and Ludacris sampled. The ultimate digger's reward.
Bossa nova composer who evolved into synth-funk pioneer. His 1980s boogie records are as futuristic as anything from New York or Detroit.
From City of God to David Bowie covers in Portuguese. Seu Jorge bridges Rio's favelas to global stages with warmth and dignity.
São Paulo artist reviving Tropicália's spirit for a new generation. Acoustic, dreamy, deeply rooted in Brazilian musical tradition while looking forward.

The father of Ethio-jazz. Studied at Berklee, returned to Addis Ababa, and created an entirely new musical language by fusing jazz with Ethiopian pentatonic scales. Broken Flowers made him famous to new ears — the music was always this deep.

Invented Afrobeat. Full stop. Every track in the global/Afrobeat section of this list exists because Fela fused James Brown's funk with Yoruba rhythms and radical politics into 20-minute protest anthems.

The swing. The off-kilter drums. The warmth. Every beat-driven artist on this list — from Makaya McCraven to Alfa Mist to Kaytranada — is working in the rhythmic space Dilla opened. Donuts is the Rosetta Stone.

Spiritual jazz that literally transcends. Her harp and organ work created a sonic vocabulary for divine searching that Angel Bat Dawid, Carlos Niño, and Flying Lotus (her grandnephew) continue to speak.
Invented ambient music as a category. Every electronic artist on this list who values texture and space over beats is building in the architecture Eno designed.

tracks.track: The Revolution Will Not Be Televised
tracks.why: Proto-rapper, jazz poet, political prophet. The direct line from Gil to the conscious hip-hop Rhythm Lab champions — Noname, billy woods, Moor Mother — is unbroken.
South London's anonymous ghost — turned UK garage's skeleton into something haunted and beautiful. Every moody, rain-soaked electronic track owes Burial a debt.
The supreme crate digger. Madvillainy, Beat Konducta, Shades of Blue — he treats the entire history of recorded music as source material. The curatorial ethos of Rhythm Lab in producer form.
Endtroducing proved an entire album could be built from samples alone. The philosophical foundation for sample-based electronic music and beat culture.
The high priestess. Mama's Gun connected Billie Holiday to J Dilla to Sun Ra in one album. Every neo-soul and experimental R&B artist on this list exists in her gravitational field.

Richard D. James made electronic music that was simultaneously alien and deeply human. Selected Ambient Works is the benchmark for electronic music with emotional depth — the quality Rhythm Lab demands from every electronic track.

HIP-HOP.description: Production-forward, lyrical depth, boundary-pushing. Not mainstream trap.

SOUL / NEO-SOUL / R&B.description: Textured, warm, vocalist-driven. Modern soul honoring tradition while pushing forward.

JAZZ / JAZZ FUSION (Contemporary).description: Modern, genre-bending, alive. Players who grew up on J Dilla AND John Coltrane.

ELECTRONIC / DANCE.description: Deep, soulful, textured. Broken beat, house with soul, bass music with musical depth.

AFROBEAT / GLOBAL / WORLD.description: Diaspora connections, rhythmic depth, cultural bridges across the global South.

CROSS-GENRE / UNCLASSIFIABLE.description: The Rhythm Lab sweet spot — artists who refuse categorization.

CANONICAL / TOUCHSTONES.description: North star artists whose influence runs through the entire list. Björk, Herbie, Dilla, Fela, Alice Coltrane, etc.

70s JAZZ FUSION.description: The fusionists who proved jazz could groove. Electric keyboards, funk rhythms, cosmic ambition.

GLOBAL GROOVES — JAPAN.description: City pop, ambient, piano jazz, YMO lineage. Tokyo sophistication and Sapporo intimacy.

GLOBAL GROOVES — GERMANY.description: Krautrock to techno to minimal. German precision meets cosmic exploration.

GLOBAL GROOVES — ITALY.description: Library music, Schema Records jazz, Neapolitan disco. Mediterranean warmth and cinematic flair.

GLOBAL GROOVES — FRANCE.description: Chanson to avant-garde to tropical soul. French artistic provocation and elegance.

GLOBAL GROOVES — BRAZIL.description: Tropicália, samba-rock, Brazilian boogie, Ethio-jazz connections. Far Out Recordings territory.

usage_notes.for_curator_agent: When evaluating a new track, ask: (1) Does it share sonic DNA with 3+ tracks on this list? (2) Does it exhibit qualities described in the WHY fields? (3) Would it create an interesting programming transition from one seed track to another? (4) Does the artist's origin story or label affiliation signal Rhythm Lab compatibility?
usage_notes.weighting: CANONICAL/TOUCHSTONES tracks carry the most weight — they define the taste ceiling. Contemporary tracks define the current programming window. 70s FUSION and GLOBAL GROOVES tracks define the historical and geographic range.
usage_notes.discovery_priority: The agent should prioritize finding NEW artists who exhibit seed list qualities over recommending established seed list artists. The seed list trains taste; the agent's job is to apply that taste to discovery.
usage_notes.milwaukee_flag: Always flag Milwaukee, Wisconsin artists and connections. Even loose connections (toured through Milwaukee, collaborated with Milwaukee artists, played Summerfest) are worth noting.
